title: Address to the 78th Session of the United Nations General Assembly
occasion: 78th Session of the United Nations General Assembly

paragraphs.text: In the name of God, the Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful. Mr. President, distinguished delegates, ladies and gentlemen. I speak to you today on behalf of a great and ancient civilization, a nation that has endured centuries of foreign interference and yet stands sovereign, independent, and determined to chart its own course in the world.

The Islamic Republic of Iran believes firmly in the principles enshrined in the Charter of this organization: sovereign equality, non-interference in internal affairs, and the peaceful settlement of disputes. These are not abstract ideals for us. They are the foundation upon which we have built our foreign policy for more than four decades.

I must begin by addressing the most consequential injustice imposed upon our nation: the regime of unilateral sanctions enforced by the United States and its allies. These sanctions are not targeted measures against individuals. They are a form of collective punishment directed at eighty-five million Iranian citizens. They restrict our access to medicine, to food, to financial systems, and to the global economy. They are, in every meaningful sense, economic warfare waged against a civilian population.

The United States withdrew from the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action — a multilateral agreement endorsed by this very Security Council — and reimposed sanctions in violation of its commitments. Iran honored its obligations under the JCPOA. This was verified repeatedly by the International Atomic Energy Agency. And yet it was Iran that paid the price for American unilateralism. What lesson does this teach the nations of the world about the value of diplomacy and negotiated agreements?

Iran’s nuclear program is and has always been peaceful. We have issued a fatwa — a religious decree of the highest authority — prohibiting the development, production, stockpiling, and use of nuclear weapons. No nation in this hall can point to a more authoritative and binding commitment against nuclear weapons. And yet the country in this region that actually possesses nuclear weapons, that has never signed the Non-Proliferation Treaty, faces no sanctions, no inspections, and no accountability.

The question of Palestine remains the central issue of justice in our region and, indeed, in the world. For seventy-five years, the Palestinian people have been subjected to occupation, dispossession, and systematic violations of their most basic rights. Their land has been confiscated, their homes demolished, their children imprisoned. The international community has passed countless resolutions, and not one has been implemented. This is not a failure of language. It is a failure of will.

Iran supports the right of the Palestinian people to self-determination and the establishment of an independent Palestinian state with Al-Quds al-Sharif as its capital. We believe that a referendum involving all original inhabitants of Palestine — Muslims, Christians, and Jews — is the most just and democratic path to resolving this conflict. This is not a radical proposal. It is the application of the very democratic principles that Western nations claim to champion.

The security of our region — West Asia and the Persian Gulf — must be determined by the nations of the region, not by external powers. The presence of foreign military bases and naval fleets in our neighborhood is not a source of stability. It is a source of tension and instability. Iran has proposed a regional security framework based on dialogue, non-aggression, and mutual respect. We are ready to engage with all our neighbors to build a more secure and prosperous region.

Iran’s relationship with its neighbors has entered a new and constructive chapter. The restoration of diplomatic relations with the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, facilitated by the People’s Republic of China, demonstrates that dialogue and mutual respect can overcome decades of tension. We welcome this development and are committed to expanding our cooperation with all nations of the region. Regional problems require regional solutions.

The world is witnessing a fundamental shift in the international order. The unipolar moment that followed the Cold War has given way to a multipolar reality. New centers of economic and political power are emerging. The nations of the Global South are demanding their rightful place in international decision-making. Iran stands firmly with this movement. We are an active member of BRICS, the Shanghai Cooperation Organization, and other platforms that represent this new reality.

Despite the immense pressure of sanctions, Iran’s economy has demonstrated remarkable resilience. We have diversified our economic base, developed domestic industries, and expanded our trade relationships with nations across Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Our knowledge-based economy is growing rapidly. Our young scientists and engineers are producing innovations in fields from biotechnology to artificial intelligence. Sanctions have imposed costs, but they have also catalyzed self-reliance.

The issue of human rights is too important to be used as a political weapon. We reject the selective and politically motivated approach to human rights that characterizes Western policy. Nations that have invaded sovereign countries, maintained secret prisons, and armed authoritarian regimes have no moral standing to lecture others. Iran is committed to the dignity and rights of its citizens within the framework of its own legal system and cultural values.

Terrorism remains a grave threat to international peace and security. Iran has been at the forefront of the fight against Daesh and other terrorist organizations. Our commanders and soldiers have sacrificed their lives in this struggle. General Qasem Soleimani, who was assassinated in a brazen act of state terrorism by the United States, was the most effective commander in the fight against Daesh. His assassination was a violation of international law and the sovereignty of Iraq.

Climate change and environmental degradation are challenges that transcend borders. Iran, despite facing water scarcity and desertification, is committed to environmental protection and sustainable development. However, climate action must be based on the principle of common but differentiated responsibilities. Developing nations cannot be expected to bear the same burden as those who industrialized by exploiting the planet’s resources for centuries.

The governance of the internet and digital technologies must respect the sovereignty of nations. Cyberattacks against Iran’s critical infrastructure, including our nuclear facilities, represent a new form of aggression that must be addressed by the international community. The weaponization of cyberspace threatens the security of all nations and requires the development of binding international norms and agreements.

Iran is a nation of culture, of poetry, of philosophy. We are the inheritors of a civilization that gave the world algebra, medicine, and architecture. We are a people who value knowledge, art, and human connection. We seek engagement with the world, not isolation. We seek cooperation, not confrontation. But we will not compromise our sovereignty, our dignity, or our right to determine our own future.

I call upon the nations gathered here to recommit to the principles of the United Nations Charter. Let us replace coercion with cooperation, sanctions with dialogue, and military threats with diplomacy. The Iranian people desire peace, prosperity, and friendship with all nations. We extend our hand in good faith and await a response in kind. Thank you, Mr. President.
